The variability estimated (shown as SD) of the environmental impacts from the Price sensitivity analysis were of the same magnitude or larger than the mean difference in environmental impacts between 2000 and 2017. This result meant that the impact of the trends in animal performance could not be observed when

variability in ingredient prices was taken into account. This is contrary to the sensitivity analysis on animal performance, which for most three- year periods had a lower SD in environmental impact than the mean difference in the environmental impacts between 2000 and 2017. This suggested that the price of the feed ingredients, which reduced the concentration of soymeal in the feed, had a large effect on the envi- ronmental impacts, and that this effect in reducing the environmental impacts could be observed irrespective of variations to the trends in animal performance.
The sensitivity analyses in this study were not designed to test the true causal cause of historical changes in the environmental impacts, but

Fig. 7. The change in the environmental impacts over the study period as a mean and standard deviation of model outcomes with sampled correlated animal performance for 100 iterations (Performance sensitivity analysis). The impacts are: Global Warming Potential (GWP), Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), Freshwater Eutrophication Potential (FEP), Agricultural Land Use (ALU) and Fossil Resource Scarcity (FRS).

only to investigate the sensitivity of trends to important input variables. Comparing the change in the mean with the SD can only indicate which input variables were most important between the two model compo- nents we examined. As such, one cannot definitively state that either changes in feed prices were the sole driver of reductions in environ- mental impacts, or that improved pig performance did not contribute to this development.
4.4. Comparison of the methodologies of other historical livestock LCA studies
Previous studies have indicated substantial reductions in

environmental impacts from pork (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Matlock et al., 2014; Putman et al., 2018; Vergé et al., 2009a; Watson et al., 2018), poultry (Vergé et al., 2009b) and egg production systems (Pelletier, 2018; Pelletier et al., 2014) over periods of several decades. Historical LCA studies of livestock systems need to estimate changes in animal performance, feed composition and manure nutrient content to make comparisons at different points in time. In the afore- mentioned studies, animal performance was taken from different pro- duction systems representing 'typical' farms (Matlock et al., 2014; Putman et al., 2018) or national databases (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009a; Watson et al., 2018). These studies derived feed compositions from either national statistics (Cederberg

et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009a), estimated them from recommendation for the period considered (Boyd et al., 2012; Putman et al., 2018) or based them on expert opinion (Watson al., 2018). Lastly, these studies estimated emissions from manure either from statistics for the periods considered (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Putman et al., 2018), simple calculations based on feed intake and composition (Vergé et al., 2009a) or mass balance principles (Watson et al., 2018). All above studies made specific inventories for the upstream feed production for the investigated years, accounting for changes in crop yields, fertilizer application and other key factors that determine the environmental impact of animal feed.

Our model was designed to test our hypothesis that changes in ani- mal performance, such as breeding for improved efficiency have resul- ted in reductions in the environmental impacts of pig systems. This test was applied to systems for which there were sparse available data on animal performance, feed composition and manure nutrient content. Given this context, we took different methodological approaches compared to the previous historical LCA studies; the advantages and limitations associated with methodological differences are discussed below.

I) We used a constant life cycle inventory for the production of feed ingredients and other upstream processes in the LCA model. For our purposes, this approach was preferable as it allowed us to eliminate changes and improvements in how feed is produced, especially over the relatively short time interval considered (<20 years). Due to this com- bination of methodological choice and limitation in the available data, our model is likely to be conservative in its presentation of the overall reduction in the environmental impacts from pig production systems. This is because it does not account for improvements in the efficiency of production for crops and other important feed inputs to pig production systems.

II) In contrast to basing feed compositions on national statistics or official recommendations, we calculated feed compositions based on least cost formulations using outputs of the animal requirement sub- model. In determining feed composition through least cost, we assumed that the pig producer is only interested in cost reduction when designing the feed. In reality, the farmer also has some concerns over animal health and risk management (James, 2018), which are not accounted for by this method. However, the approaches of previous studies also have their limitations. For instance, feed compositions based on national statistics are difficult to apply to individual phases in the life cycle of the pig, since these data are often not available. As such, esti- mating feed composition based on national statistics makes it very difficult to estimate the manure nutrient content accurately. We suggest that the approach taken here is a reasonable trade-off between limita- tions and consequences.
58: <header>4.2. Trends in of the environmental impacts of pig production </header>

All predicted environmental impacts decreased over time, which was consistent with our hypothesis that breeding trends have improved an- imal performance over time and thus reduced the environmental im- pacts of the system per functional unit. The degree of reduction varied between the environmental impact categories considered. As the ma- jority of the environmental impacts were associated with the finisher component of the system, any reductions were mainly attributed to three main factors: 1) change in animal performance, which led (mainly) to a decrease in energy concentration of feed; 2) change in feed ingre- dient prices and inclusion of alternative, home grown ingredients in the; and 3) increase in slaughter weight without an increase in inputs. Especially changes in 1) and 2) resulted in changes in feed composition, which drove the changes in the environmental impact of the systems considered.

GWP was the most sensitive impact category due to its high depen- dence on the inclusion of soybean meal in the feed, especially for the production pigs. Soybean is imported in the UK and comes mainly from South America; as a consequence it has a high GWP impact per kg of ingredient due to deforestation associated with its production and transport (Pre Consultants, 2017). Any reductions in its inclusion over time, as was the case here, would automatically lead to reduced GWP impacts. Reductions in the TAP and FEP impacts over time were more moderate, reflecting the fact that changes in these impact categories over time were minimal for the production pigs. The main contributors to the reduction in ALU were the higher inclusions of co-products and reduced CFI, which led to smaller relative reductions than in the other impact categories. The FRS impact category is more affected by in- gredients with high energy inputs to processing (Mackenzie et al., 2016), ingredients such as animal co-product, pure amino acid and rapeseed

Fig. 5. Contributions from the different pig production phases to Global Warming Potential (GWP), Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), Freshwater Eutro- phication Potential (FEP), Agricultural Land Use (ALU) and Fossil Resource Scarcity (FRS) from indoor and outdoor pig production in Great Britain. The piglet phase contains contributions from the full reproductive phase. Since indoor bred and outdoor bred early and late weaners are indistinguishable in the figure, only the indoor bred weaners are shown.
meal are associated with high levels of FRS. The reductions in FRS due to decreases in cumulative feed intake were partly counterbalanced by the increasing inclusion of rapeseed meal over time.

In the reproduction component of the system, an increased litter size would be expected to reduce its environmental impacts. However, this was partly counter-balanced by an increase in the SOW replacement rates and therefore only limited reductions in the environmental impact of this system component were seen. There were only small differences in the environmental impacts between the indoor and outdoor breeding component of the system, even though outdoor breeding requires more energy inputs. This can be attributed to the disproportionate higher feed
intake and thereby lower energy concentration in the feed of outdoor SOWS compared to indoor SOWS.

The reproductive phase contributed more to the TAP impact of the system, than for any other impact category. The breeding SOW has significantly lower protein requirements than the production pig, which necessitates the inclusion of high energy, but low protein feed in- gredients. However, as the feed formulation was based on least cost, the resulting feed included high energy, but also relatively high protein ingredients, which is a usual challenge in the formulation of SOW feed (Edwards et al., 2002). This resulted in oversupply of nitrogen in the feed, which led to over excretion of nitrogen in the manure from this

The main contributors to the reduction in ALU were the higher inclusions of co-products and reduced CFI, which led to smaller relative reductions than in the other impact categories. The FRS impact category is more affected by in- gredients with high energy inputs to processing (Mackenzie et al., 2016), ingredients such as animal co-product, pure amino acid and rapeseed

The piglet phase contains contributions from the full reproductive phase. meal are associated with high levels of FRS. The reductions in FRS due to decreases in cumulative feed intake were partly counterbalanced by the increasing inclusion of rapeseed meal over time.

In the reproduction component of the system, an increased litter size would be expected to reduce its environmental impacts. However, this was partly counter-balanced by an increase in the SOW replacement rates and therefore only limited reductions in the environmental impact of this system component were seen. There were only small differences in the environmental impacts between the indoor and outdoor breeding component of the system, even though outdoor breeding requires more energy inputs. This can be attributed to the disproportionate higher feed
intake and thereby lower energy concentration in the feed of outdoor SOWS compared to indoor SOWS.

The reproductive phase contributed more to the TAP impact of the system, than for any other impact category. The breeding SOW has significantly lower protein requirements than the production pig, which necessitates the inclusion of high energy, but low protein feed in- gredients. However, as the feed formulation was based on least cost, the resulting feed included high energy, but also relatively high protein ingredients, which is a usual challenge in the formulation of SOW feed (Edwards et al., 2002). This resulted in oversupply of nitrogen in the feed, which led to over excretion of nitrogen in the manure from this

Fig. 6. The change in the environmental impacts over the study period as a mean and standard deviation of model outcomes with constant prices set to each in- dividual year (Price sensitivity analysis). The impacts are: Global Warming Potential (GWP), Terrestrial Acidification Potential (TAP), Freshwater Eutrophication Potential (FEP), Agricultural Land Use (ALU) and Fossil Resource Scarcity (FRS).

The proposed model had two main sources of data that contributed with substantial uncertainty: the feed ingredient prices and animal performance data. The variability estimated (shown as SD) of the environmental impacts from the Price sensitivity analysis were of the same magnitude or larger than the mean difference in environmental impacts between 2000 and 2017. This result meant that the impact of the trends in animal performance could not be observed when

variability in ingredient prices was taken into account. This is contrary to the sensitivity analysis on animal performance, which for most three- year periods had a lower SD in environmental impact than the mean difference in the environmental impacts between 2000 and 2017. This suggested that the price of the feed ingredients, which reduced the concentration of soymeal in the feed, had a large effect on the envi- ronmental impacts, and that this effect in reducing the environmental impacts could be observed irrespective of variations to the trends in animal performance.
The sensitivity analyses in this study were not designed to test the true causal cause of historical changes in the environmental impacts, but

Fig. 7. The change in the environmental impacts over the study period as a mean and standard deviation of model outcomes with sampled correlated animal performance for 100 iterations (Performance sensitivity analysis). only to investigate the sensitivity of trends to important input variables. Comparing the change in the mean with the SD can only indicate which input variables were most important between the two model compo- nents we examined. As such, one cannot definitively state that either changes in feed prices were the sole driver of reductions in environ- mental impacts, or that improved pig performance did not contribute to this development.
4.4. Comparison of the methodologies of other historical livestock LCA studies
Previous studies have indicated substantial reductions in

environmental impacts from pork (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Matlock et al., 2014; Putman et al., 2018; Vergé et al., 2009a; Watson et al., 2018), poultry (Vergé et al., 2009b) and egg production systems (Pelletier, 2018; Pelletier et al., 2014) over periods of several decades. Historical LCA studies of livestock systems need to estimate changes in animal performance, feed composition and manure nutrient content to make comparisons at different points in time. In the afore- mentioned studies, animal performance was taken from different pro- duction systems representing 'typical' farms (Matlock et al., 2014; Putman et al., 2018) or national databases (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009a; Watson et al., 2018). These studies derived feed compositions from either national statistics (Cederberg

et al., 2009; Vergé et al., 2009a), estimated them from recommendation for the period considered (Boyd et al., 2012; Putman et al., 2018) or based them on expert opinion (Watson al., 2018). Lastly, these studies estimated emissions from manure either from statistics for the periods considered (Boyd et al., 2012; Cederberg et al., 2009; Putman et al., 2018), simple calculations based on feed intake and composition (Vergé et al., 2009a) or mass balance principles (Watson et al., 2018). All above studies made specific inventories for the upstream feed production for the investigated years, accounting for changes in crop yields, fertilizer application and other key factors that determine the environmental impact of animal feed.

Our model was designed to test our hypothesis that changes in ani- mal performance, such as breeding for improved efficiency have resul- ted in reductions in the environmental impacts of pig systems. This test was applied to systems for which there were sparse available data on animal performance, feed composition and manure nutrient content. Given this context, we took different methodological approaches compared to the previous historical LCA studies; the advantages and limitations associated with methodological differences are discussed below.

I) We used a constant life cycle inventory for the production of feed ingredients and other upstream processes in the LCA model. For our purposes, this approach was preferable as it allowed us to eliminate changes and improvements in how feed is produced, especially over the relatively short time interval considered (<20 years). Due to this com- bination of methodological choice and limitation in the available data, our model is likely to be conservative in its presentation of the overall reduction in the environmental impacts from pig production systems. This is because it does not account for improvements in the efficiency of production for crops and other important feed inputs to pig production systems.

II) In contrast to basing feed compositions on national statistics or official recommendations, we calculated feed compositions based on least cost formulations using outputs of the animal requirement sub- model. In determining feed composition through least cost, we assumed that the pig producer is only interested in cost reduction when designing the feed. In reality, the farmer also has some concerns over animal health and risk management (James, 2018), which are not accounted for by this method. However, the approaches of previous studies also have their limitations. For instance, feed compositions based on national statistics are difficult to apply to individual phases in the life cycle of the pig, since these data are often not available. As such, esti- mating feed composition based on national statistics makes it very difficult to estimate the manure nutrient content accurately. We suggest that the approach taken here is a reasonable trade-off between limita- tions and consequences.
62: <header>4.2. Trends in of the environmental impacts of pig production </header>

All predicted environmental impacts decreased over time, which was consistent with our hypothesis that breeding trends have improved an- imal performance over time and thus reduced the environmental im- pacts of the system per functional unit. The degree of reduction varied between the environmental impact categories considered. As the ma- jority of the environmental impacts were associated with the finisher component of the system, any reductions were mainly attributed to three main factors: 1) change in animal performance, which led (mainly) to a decrease in energy concentration of feed; 2) change in feed ingre- dient prices and inclusion of alternative, home grown ingredients in the; and 3) increase in slaughter weight without an increase in inputs. Especially changes in 1) and 2) resulted in changes in feed composition, which drove the changes in the environmental impact of the systems considered.

GWP was the most sensitive impact category due to its high depen- dence on the inclusion of soybean meal in the feed, especially for the production pigs. Soybean is imported in the UK and comes mainly from South America; as a consequence it has a high GWP impact per kg of ingredient due to deforestation associated with its production and transport (Pre Consultants, 2017). Any reductions in its inclusion over time, as was the case here, would automatically lead to reduced GWP impacts. Reductions in the TAP and FEP impacts over time were more moderate, reflecting the fact that changes in these impact categories over time were minimal for the production pigs. The main contributors to the reduction in ALU were the higher inclusions of co-products and reduced CFI, which led to smaller relative reductions than in the other impact categories. The FRS impact category is more affected by in- gredients with high energy inputs to processing (Mackenzie et al., 2016), ingredients such as animal co-product, pure amino acid and rapeseed

63: We estimated that GB pig production systems, indoor and outdoor bred respectively, reduced GWP by 37.0% and 35.4%, TAP by 21.2% and 16.4%, FEP by 22.5% and 22.3%, ALU by 15.8% and 16.8% and FRS by 16.5% and 16.1% over an 18-year period. Changes in feed ingredient prices, which reduce high-impact ingredient inclusion, were an impor- tant factor in determining these reductions and the outcomes of the LCA model were very sensitive to these. Change in performance and thereby animal nutrient resource requirements also contributed to the re- ductions in environmental impacts over time although trends in these data were less sensitive inputs to the model than feed composition. The method presented here for historical livestock LCA, where performance data is used to estimate requirements, feed composition and environ- mental impacts, can be applied to any animal system in the past, present and future, as long as data on animal performance and ingredient prices are available. This approach will give researchers opportunities to es- timate environmental impacts for systems with sparse data availability, identify the main contributors to any changes in environmental impacts

and propose improvements for systems in a less expensive and less time consuming manner. Further, this method might be an alternative strat- egy for studies on national environmental impact of different livestock systems. Currently, such studies rely heavily on registration of feed composition and intake, which are not always reliable at a national level.
